covid_lungs: Chest X-ray image showing lungs affected by COVID-19, characterized by bilateral, peripheral ground-glass opacities.
COVID-19 lungs often display patchy consolidations and interstitial markings, especially in the lower lobes.
The image may reveal multifocal infiltrates and a hazy appearance typical of viral pneumonia.
COVID-19 infection can cause diffuse lung involvement with areas of increased density.
This class is defined by radiographic features consistent with SARS-CoV-2 infection.
lung_opacity_lungs: Chest X-ray image showing increased lung opacity, which may be due to various causes such as infection, inflammation, or edema.
Lung opacity appears as areas of whiteness or cloudiness, obscuring normal lung markings.
The image may reveal focal or diffuse opacities, which can be unilateral or bilateral.
Lung opacities are non-specific and require clinical correlation for diagnosis.
This class is characterized by abnormal radiodensity within the lung fields.
normal_lungs: Chest X-ray image of healthy lungs, showing clear lung fields with no evidence of disease.
Normal lungs appear as well-aerated, with sharp costophrenic angles and visible vascular markings.
There are no signs of consolidation, opacity, or abnormal infiltrates in a normal lung image.
The image serves as a reference for comparison with pathological chest X-rays.
Normal lung X-rays reveal intact lung structure and regular radiographic patterns.
viral_pneumonia_lungs: Chest X-ray image showing lungs affected by viral pneumonia, with patchy or diffuse infiltrates.
Viral pneumonia lungs often display bilateral ground-glass opacities and interstitial changes.
The image may reveal areas of consolidation and increased lung markings, especially in the periphery.
Viral pneumonia can cause multifocal involvement and a hazy appearance on chest X-ray.
This class is defined by radiographic features typical of viral lung infections, excluding COVID-19.
